observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct an annual physical inventory-taking of all PPE and submit a complete RPCPPE within the deadline;
Direct the Designated GSO to practice the periodic reconciliation with the accounting records to immediately detect errors/irregularities;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the completed/acquired PPE booked in the Trust Fund to the General Fund books, ensure recognition of depreciation expense of all the PPE accounts, and record the adjustments in the government equity account for the prior years’ depreciation expenses; and
Direct the Inventory Committee to exert utmost effort in conducting the activities relative to the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances.
Management Comment:
During the exit conference, the Designated GSO and Municipal Accountant promised that best efforts will be exerted to conduct physical count of all PPE and perform reconciliation with their respective records.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly account for the acquisition and issuance of the supplies and materials inventory based on IPSAS 12, Inventories, and make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balance of the affected inventory and expense accounts. Also, make sure that the supporting documents for every transaction are complete and that the necessary details are properly filled out;
Require the Designated GSO to request the end user to submit RIS prior the issuance of supplies and submit to the Accounting Unit on a weekly basis the SSMI as basis for taking up the expense and conduct periodic physical count of supplies and materials on hand and reconcile the same with the accounting records as often as necessary; and
Instruct the BAC, upon receipt of the PR, to ensure that only the relevant specifications and performance requirements of the goods/materials to be purchased are indicated in the PR without indicating the brand names.
Management Comment:
Management ensured that the audit recommendations will be complied with.

We recommended that Management:
Require the OIC – Municipal Treasurer to strictly deposit all his collections within the period required by law and regulations; and
Designate a Liquidating Officer to acknowledge remittances from collectors and deposit the same to the government depository bank.
Management Comment:
During the exit conference, Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Prioritize issuance of demand letter to all AOs and employees to immediately liquidate/settle their outstanding cash advances;
File a request for authority to write-off dormant or long outstanding unliquidated cash advances, if warranted, to the Office of the Auditor; and
Continue to strengthen the operation of preventive and monitoring controls to ensure timely submission of liquidation documents and full accounting of balances and avoid undue accumulation of outstanding cash advances.
Management Comment:
Management commented that they are having difficulty in providing the required supporting documents for the request for write off of those unliquidated cash advances which are considered dormant. However, they agreed and promised to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that:
The LGU, through the RHU, Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) and Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) design its own proactive PPAs and provide funds therefor to curtail, eliminate,  and stop the resurgence of the deadly COVID-19 pursuant to existing laws and issuances;
The MHO is requested to submit duly certified list of health workers assigned during the LGU vaccination activities that were granted the provisions of foods for further audit verification; and
The Municipal Budget Officer and LDRRMO submit an explanation on the disbursements charged against the LDRRMF for COVID-19 response without corresponding approved budget.
Management Comment:
Management commented that they have focused on managing the COVID-19 crisis, and overlooked some of the requirements and was not able to comply with pertinent rules and regulations on the budgeting and disbursement of funds. During the exit conference, Management explained that the identified COVID-19 PPAs were duly funded. However, the budget for such PPAs was presented in lump sum. Management admitted that they should have specified the PPAs to be charged per fund source for proper and accurate monitoring and reporting. Nevertheless, they have committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that:
The LGU, through the BAC, MPDO and Municipal Engineer, to effectively plan and implement immediately all developmental projects within the calendar year in order that stakeholders may not be deprived from the benefits and enjoyment of government projects.  All planned developmental projects must be aligned with the provincial, regional, and national developmental projects for efficiency, effectiveness, and economy of its implementation;
The BAC must strictly adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184, its Revised IRR and other applicable laws and regulations in the evaluation of the bidders;
The LGU must submit Appraisal Report on the purchase of dump truck to establish the reasonable acquisition cost of the unit based on the actual conditions of the reconditioned truck.  Also, the supplier must submit LTO Accreditation Permit as Dealer/Seller of motor vehicle as required by laws and regulation on the matter; and
If the infrastructure work is satisfactorily done and on schedule, stop the practice of retaining 10 per cent from progress billing after 50 per cent completion, instead, require contractors to post Warranty Security for all completed and accepted projects.  The Warranty Security is also applied to the delivery and acceptance of the reconditioned Dump Truck for a period of one year.
Management Comment:
Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations. Further, the BAC promised to strictly observe the procurement process and be more cautious on the correctness and completeness of the documents submitted.

We recommended that:
The annual LFRRMFIP must be aligned with the Municipality’s 6-year Comprehensive-Integrated Disaster Plan.  Only those PPAs which were embodied in the aforesaid plan must be given due course in the formulation of annual Municipal DRRMFIP;
The LDRRMC must monitor the implementation of disaster related PPAs and evaluate the use of fund thereof to avoid disbursement of ineligible PPAs using the LDRRMF. Implement immediately all planned disaster related PPAs for better disaster risk reduction and management; and
Management submit a satisfactory explanation on those disbursements without approved budget and/or not incorporated in the approved LDRRMFIP and annual LDRRM Plan and Budget.
Management Comment:
Management commented that the preparation of the Comprehensive-Integrated Disaster Plan is underway and the implementation of some of the planned disaster-related PPAs is ongoing. Further, Management noted the audit recommendations and ensured their compliance.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the RHU to look into the causes why claims for Maternal Care Package were not filed with the PhilHealth within the regulatory period;
Require the Rural Health Officer to submit explanation as to why the claims were not filed with the PhilHealth;
Together with the RHU, revisit the Agreement with PhilHealth on Capitation Fund including the compliance with the requirements as enunciated under PhilHealth regulation, henceforth, exert effort to avail for the payments thereof; and
Assign personnel task solely for the processing and filing of PhilHealth claims and provide such personnel with appropriate resources for use in face to face and/or online filing, as the case maybe.
Management Comment:
Management commented that PhilHealth claims were not filed within the prescribed period due to various reasons such as change of benefit package which needed some requirements that took a while to be complied, unstable internet connection and lack of equipment needed for the uploading of data, and the status of accreditation is still on process. Nonetheless, Management committed that they are doing their best to comply all the needed requirements and documents to improve their work and provide better services to their community.

We recommended the following:
Stop the practice of granting cash assistance to clients or person in the absence of documentary requirements. The submission of complete documentary requirements for each type of assistance are condition precedent to the payment; and
Individuals seeking medical assistance must be endorsed to the RHU of the LGU for their disposition rather than providing them immediate cash assistance.  Indigent persons are covered by the National Health Insurance Program of the government by enrolling them to PhilHealth under the Point Of Care (POC) enrolment policy.  Only when the RHU has no available medicines for the client/patient, the medical assistance may be granted subject to the guidelines enunciated by the DSWD MC 11, s. 2019.
Management Comment:
Management promised to comply with the audit recommendations and adhere to laws and regulations in the grant of cash assistance. Further, lacking supporting documents were partially submitted after the exit conference.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget Officer to exert more effort in preparing and submitting on time the accounts and financial reports including the supporting documents and schedules. We request that such activities be given utmost priority for timely and better fiscal reporting.
Management Comment:
Management acknowledged the deficiencies noted and agreed to comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the disbursements for repairs made by the Municipality are supported with complete and appropriate documentation as prescribed in Section 9.1.3.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 362 Volume I of GAAM;
Direct the Designated GSO to submit the Pre-repair Evaluation Reports and the Post-inspection Reports relative to the above-mentioned transactions and other homogenous disbursements; and
Require that Certificates of Acceptance and RWMs be prepared by the Designated GSO and approved by the Local Chief Executive, in case of major repairs and if the repairs need replacement.
Management Comment:
Management assured the Audit Team that recommendations will be complied with.

We recommended that Management stop immediately the practice of including the payments of loan amortizations to private loan entities as deduction from the payroll and performing the collection and monitoring activities for them. Officials and employees who availed loans from these private entities must personally settle their obligations directly with the bank.
Management Comment:
Management expressed strong commitment for the immediate compliance of the other audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that:

a. The Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the BRS, together with the
copies of the BS and supporting documents for the reconciling items,
within the prescribed period for a timely review/verification of such report
and prepare SLs per bank account;

b. The Municipal Treasurer prepare and update the Cash in Bank cashbooks
for all accounts regularly;

c. The Municipal Treasurer close the two bank accounts which are already
dormant; and

d. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer review, analyze and
reconcile their respective records (GL and Cashbook) to determine the
nature of each account and come up with the correct balances of the Cash
in Bank accounts for all funds.

We recommended that Management:

a. Immediately send demand letters to officials and employees, who have
outstanding cash advances as of December 31, 2020, to settle the overdue
amounts within a specific period. Otherwise, effect the withholding of their
salaries pursuant to 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 until such time that
the cash advances are fully settled;

b. Prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for the liquidation of the cash
advances for Intelligence/Confidential Fund which already have
transmittal letter duly received by COA Central Office thru COA –
Intelligence and Confidential Audit Unit (ICFAU); and

c. Strengthen the operation of preventive and monitoring controls to ensure
timely submission of liquidation documents and full accounting of balances
and avoid undue accumulation of outstanding cash advances.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendations that:

a. The Designated Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) and
Municipal Accountant prioritize the reconciliation of the results of the
inventory count with the property and accounting records, look into the
noted discrepancies and effect/prepare the necessary adjusting entries, as
the case may be;

b. The Municipal Accountant exhaust all efforts to fully identify the PPE with
lump sum balances and the Local Chief Executive file the request for
authority to derecognize non-existing/missing PPE, duly supported with
the required documents, if warranted; and

c. The Municipal Accountant immediately draw a JEV to reclassify the semi-
expendable items and the related Accumulated Depreciation to the
appropriate accounts and thereafter, observe the ₱15,000 capitalization
threshold when classifying/recording a tangible item as PPE. Also, issue
the corresponding Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) to establish
accountability over these tangible items which were already issued to the
end-users.

We recommended that:

a. The Municipal Treasury Office and Budget Office, as the lead offices, and
the Local Finance Committee study thoroughly the reasonableness of the
projected income to be collected to arrive at a more realistic
appropriation; and

b. The Municipal Treasurer must conduct periodic review to check the
collection performance level and devise remedial measures for revenue
accounts that missed the projections and effective procedures that will
improve more the collections of taxes that registered good results.

We recommended that:

a. The ICO-Municipal Treasurer immediately submit the issued receipts,
paid vouchers and payrolls accounts, to the Office of the Municipal
Accountant for recording in the books of accounts, after which, the same
must be submitted to the Auditor within the first ten (10) days of the
ensuing month, together with its supporting documents; and

b. The Office of the Municipal Accountant must exert extra efforts to ensure
that the required mandatory financial reports and accounts, particularly
the year-end financial statements, be submitted within the set deadline.

We recommended that:

a. The LGU, through the concerned offices/departments, observe the prudent
fund management in its effort to curtail/eliminate/mitigate the spread of
COVID-19 in the locality. Henceforth, all PPAs related to COVID-19 must
be judiciously planned according to priorities considering the scarce
resources of the LGU and the urgent needs for health, safety, and
livelihood of the constituents;

b. Department/Units responsible for the procurement and distribution of
food relief to account for all purchases, distribution, and balance and
submit report immediately for further audit and verification and to avoid
audit disallowance;

c. Submit properly accomplished Driver’s Trip Ticket, Monthly Summary
Report of Official Travel, Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption for
further audit and validation immediately upon receipt hereof, otherwise
disbursement pertaining to aforesaid transaction shall be disallowed in audit for inability to adhere with the regulations; and

d. Observe and adhere to the required posting of APP, NOA, and PPAs
implemented by the LGU to the GPPB portal for transparency and
reportorial compliance.

We recommended that the LSB:

a. Obtain/secure the required DepEd approved SIP, DEDP and other
information from the DepEd RO on the allocation of each school from the
National Budget as contained in the NEP, which supposedly were the basis
for the preparation and approval by the LSB of the SEF Budget and
furnish the Audit Team, for reference and review purposes. Henceforth,
the LSB must strictly follow the mandated required processes and
documents in planning and budgeting for SEF under the revised guidelines
on the use of SEF;

b. Incorporate in the budget programs and activities intended for ECCD as
required under Section 7 (b) of RA No. 10410 and satisfactory explanation
is requested why ECCD was not given allocation despite the sufficient
revenue generated under 1 per cent SET; and

c. The Municipal Engineer is requested to explain the basis of the uniform
labor components in the amount of ₱9,000 for the repair and maintenance
of 32 public schools, despite the different and peculiar scope of works of
each and every project, otherwise, said expenditure shall be disallowed in
audit for improper disbursements of fund. Moreover, explanation is also
requested as to why all 32 repair projects of public schools has uniform
number of days to complete at 30 working days despite of the simple scope
of works that may be completed in just two to 10 days.

We recommended that:

a. The LGU, strictly observe the laws and regulations on the budgeting and
utilization of LDRRMF by utilizing the fund only for those allowed and
legitimate PPAs to avoid depleting the fund for the expenditures outside
the intent for its authorization;

b. Explanation is requested as to why Section 4(u) of RA No. 11494, DBM
LBC No. 124, and Item 3 of GPPB Resolution No. 18-2020 were not
observed for the budgetary realignment of fund, procurements and
disbursements, and the required posting to GPPB portal and other places;

c. Submission of satisfactory explanation and additional documents, if
warranted to justify the excessive price on the procurement of medical
supplies and equipment, and the reports, accounting, and disposal of rice
and diesel fuel otherwise aforesaid disbursement shall be
suspended/disallowed in audit; and

d. Submit explanation on the excess disbursements from the amount
appropriated from LDRRMF to COVID-19 related PPAs, otherwise the
same shall be disallowed in audit for want of budgetary authorization from
the Legislative.

We recommended that:

a. The Municipality, through its disaster officers and the council, revisit its
over-all plans for LDRRM to develop holistic and proactive plans that will
ensure for preventing, mitigating and quick recovery of the constituents in
time of disasters and emergencies and prepare a comprehensive LDRRM
Investment Plan as the basis for annual plan and budget; and

b. Refund the unallowable expenditures charged against LDRRMF
immediately and submit the required documents/reports on the payment of
diesel fuel.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point
System (GFPS) or its equivalent officers/employees to:

a. Strictly comply with the existing rules and regulations prescribing the
guidelines in the formulation of Annual GPB, development of gender
issues, GAD activities, and implementation and monitoring of the GPB.
Mainstreaming of regular PPAs must be made through the use of HGDG
assessment tools to justify the attribution of the portion or whole costs to
GAD budget;

b. Avoid the general statements of the gender issue which do not necessarily
reflect gender differences in the situation of women and men. The gender
issue must be specific and supported by evidence such as sex disaggregated
data and gender analysis results;

c. Utilize the GAD Fund only for acceptable PPAs that would address the
identified gender issues in the locality based on the RA No. 9710 or the
Magna Carta of Women, PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as
amended, and other relevant international commitments; and

d. Submit a copy of the Annual GPB to COA within the prescribed period for
the timely audit so that recommended corrective measures may also be
issued in the appropriate time.

We recommended that:

a. Implement immediately and on timely basis the identified PPAs under the
20 per cent DF. The Municipal Planning and Development Officer and
Municipal Engineer must monitor the implementation of the projects to
fast tract its completion;

b. The members of the BAC schedule early all procurement activities
pertaining to development projects to pave way for their timely
implementation and completion;

c. All 20 per cent DF projects must be effectively planned and the same shall
be procurement-and implementation-ready within the fiscal year;

d. Satisfactory explanation is requested as why procurement law and
regulations particularly GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 were not observed in
the procurement of COVID-19 related PPAs and submit immediately the
accounting of receipt and distribution of the procured food items for
further validation in audit, otherwise disbursement related to said
procurement shall be disallowed in audit; and

e. Submit FDA license of SAA Variety Store pertaining to the procurement of
medical supplies purchased from that store.

observations.recommendation: 6) We reiterated our previous years’ audit recommendation that all contracts together with its supporting documents must be submitted for review and evaluation to the Office of the Auditor within five working days upon approval. We further recommended the immediate submission of the complete set of duly approved plans and drawings to facilitate timely review and evaluation of the Municipality’s infrastructure projects.

observations.recommendation:
7) We recommended to require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to submit the required monthly Trial Balances, Financial Statements and Other Financial reports within the prescribed period.
